title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for Generic Function with Union Types of Custom Objects

statement.markdown: Implement a function `mergeObjects` that merges two objects into a new object. The function should take two custom objects of type `User` or `Admin` as input. The function should merge the properties of the two input objects into a new object. If both objects have a property with the same name, the value from the second object should overwrite the value from the first object. The function should return the merged object. Note that the `User` object has properties `name` and `email`, while the `Admin` object has properties `name`, `email`, and `isAdmin`.
statement.inputFormat: Two custom objects of type `User` or `Admin`
statement.outputFormat: A new object with the merged properties
statement.notes: Make sure to handle the union types of the custom objects correctly.

examples.input: User { name: 'John', email: 'john@example.com' }, Admin { name: 'John', email: 'john2@example.com', isAdmin: true }
examples.explanation: The function should merge the properties of the two input objects into a new object.

examples.input: User { name: 'Jane', email: 'jane@example.com' }, User { name: 'Jane', email: 'jane2@example.com' }
examples.explanation: The function should merge the properties of the two input objects into a new object.

hints.text: Use the `in` operator to check if a property exists in an object.
Use the `|` operator to specify the union types of the custom objects.

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for Generic Function with Union Types of Custom Objects
editorial.intuition: To fix the type inference issue, we need to specify the union types of the custom objects correctly.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use the `in` operator to check if a property exists in an object and then merge the properties accordingly.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a generic function to merge the properties of the two input objects.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `Object.assign` method to merge the properties of the two input objects.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by checking the type inference of the generic function.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: User { name: 'John', email: 'john@example.com' }, Admin { name: 'John', email: 'john2@example.com', isAdmin: true }
commonMistakes: Incorrectly specifying the union types of the custom objects
Not using the `in` operator to check if a property exists in an object

functionSignature.javascript: function mergeObjects<T>(obj1: T, obj2: T): T

testCases.input: User { name: 'John', email: 'john@example.com' }, Admin { name: 'John', email: 'john2@example.com', isAdmin: true }
User { name: 'Jane', email: 'jane@example.com' }, User { name: 'Jane', email: 'jane2@example.com' }
